content: Imagine you are building a real-time multiplayer drawing game where two friends on different continents need to see each other's strokes instantly without waiting for a server round-trip. This is the core motivation for using Peer connections in our Rust implementation via webrtc-rs, because traditional TCP servers introduce unacceptable latency and bottlenecks when bandwidth is high or network jitter occurs. The journey begins with establishing a signaling channel before any actual media flows; think of this as sending an email to coordinate meeting times, where your code uses the tokio runtime to handle asynchronous HTTP requests to a simple Node.js server that exchanges SDP offers and answers via the webrtc-rs Signaling trait. Once the SDP exchange is complete, you must initialize a PeerConnection instance, which internally manages ICE candidates and STUN servers like those provided by Google; without these, your browser will silently fail because it cannot discover public IP addresses or traverse NAT boundaries. The magic happens when you call set_local_description on one peer and set_remote_description on the other using the webrtc-rs API, triggering an ICE Agent that iteratively tests connectivity through direct paths, STUN reflections, and eventually TURN server relays if necessary; this is the rigorous handshake phase where your Rust code must properly handle the ICE state machine transitions to ensure both sides are actually reachable. Once the connection reaches the connected state represented by a successful webrtc::Connection object, you can then attach DataChannels to that peer for transmitting binary blobs or text strings alongside video streams, effectively allowing one user to send a new drawing shape while the other receives it in real-time without any server intervention. The final critical piece is managing the lifecycle of these connections within your tokio-based application; when a user disconnects or crashes, you must gracefully shut down the webrtc-rs connection and release any associated resources like the ICE agent or STUN/TURN credentials to prevent memory leaks, ensuring your application remains responsive even under heavy concurrent loads from multiple users trying to establish these complex peer-to-peer links simultaneously.
